Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings. I'd block out about three to four hours at a time, mostly reading through course materials and taking notes by hand. Honestly, some weeks were better than others—when I felt overwhelmed or sad, my routine would fall apart and I'd struggle to get back on track. I relied heavily on written notes and would reread them multiple times to absorb the content.
Q2: I aimed for regularity, but it definitely varied depending on how I was feeling emotionally and what deadlines were coming up. Some weeks I'd stick to my schedule perfectly, but other weeks—especially when stress hit hard—I'd find myself cramming closer to due dates. The inconsistency frustrated me because I know I do better with structure, but maintaining it felt exhausting at times.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew an assignment was due or when I needed to check announcements. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was anything new, but honestly, I wasn't someone who checked it constantly. The prompt was usually practical—needing to download readings or submit something—rather than curiosity or habit.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, logging in became more routine because I was trying to stay on top of my 20 courses. I'd check in every couple of days, usually in the morning with my coffee. But that habit faded when things got harder emotionally—the routine felt like another burden rather than something natural. It never fully became automatic for me.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were most useful because I learn best through reading and writing things down. I appreciated when instructors provided detailed text-based summaries rather than just videos. The discussion forums were somewhat helpful too—reading other students' perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with, even if I didn't always have the energy to post myself.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally engaged with video content because watching videos feels passive to me and I retain less that way. I'd sometimes let them play while doing other things, which I know isn't effective. Optional supplementary materials usually got ignored because I was already stretched thin trying to keep up with the required work across all my courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I finished something and felt like I understood the material better or could apply it, that felt valuable. But when activities seemed like busywork or repetitive, I'd lose motivation quickly—I didn't have the mental energy to spare on things that felt pointless.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required work already overwhelmed me, so optional things got pushed aside even when they seemed interesting. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but I had to prioritize what would actually affect my grade. Required activities carried weight and urgency that optional ones just didn't have.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded most of the readings and PDFs early in the semester and studied from those offline constantly. I also kept a physical notebook where I wrote summaries and key concepts—that's really where most of my learning happened. The LMS was more like a retrieval system for materials rather than where I actually did my studying.
Q10: Most of my studying happened with printed notes and my handwritten summaries spread across my desk. I'd spend hours reviewing these materials, making connections between concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere online. There were entire weekends where I studied intensively but probably didn't log into the LMS at all because I already had what I needed downloaded.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a reading once, maybe skim it online briefly, then print it or save it to my tablet and work through it multiple times over several days. My first access was just grabbing the file—the real engagement happened offline where I could highlight, annotate, and reread at my own pace without needing internet connection.
Q12: There were several points where engaging became really hard, usually when my stress levels peaked and sadness took over. Around midterms especially, I felt paralyzed—I knew I needed to study but couldn't make myself start. Life stuff outside of school compounded everything, and the CBT sessions helped somewhat but didn't fix the underlying exhaustion I felt.
Q13: Absolutely. Managing 20 online courses while dealing with high stress meant I was constantly triaging. Fatigue made me less likely to engage deeply with the LMS—I'd do the minimum to get by rather than exploring thoroughly. When I was exhausted, even logging in felt like climbing a mountain, so I'd put it off until absolutely necessary.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything registering. I'd scroll through readings or have discussion posts open, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about other things or just feeling numb. The activity technically happened, but meaningful learning wasn't occurring. It was going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was the sadness making everything feel pointless or too hard. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same heaviness. The avoidance made my anxiety worse, which made engaging even harder.
Q16: Deadlines were essentially the only thing that forced me to engage sometimes. I'd have bursts of activity right before things were due, which probably shows in my data. Without deadlines, I might have drifted entirely—they provided structure when my internal motivation couldn't. But the last-minute pressure also increased my stress significantly.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost and made me want to engage more. But when feedback was critical or when I didn't perform as well as I'd hoped, it reinforced my negative feelings and made me want to withdraw. I'd sometimes avoid checking grades because I was afraid of what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. When modules were clearly organized with explicit expectations, I could manage better. But when things were scattered or requirements were unclear, my anxiety spiked and I'd disengage. Having 20 courses meant I gravitated toward the ones with clearer structures and let the confusing ones slide.
Q19: Clear weekly checklists and well-organized reading lists made engagement easier—I knew exactly what to do. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on video content or had vague instructions. Also, courses that required lots of peer interaction were difficult because I didn't always have the emotional energy to engage with others meaningfully.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but struggling to produce work—maybe they're confused or overwhelmed and keep revisiting materials hoping something clicks. It could also be anxiety-driven, where you're checking and rechecking things but can't bring yourself to actually submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. A lot of my actual learning happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. Someone could appear inactive online but be deeply engaged with the content in ways the system can't see. Performance comes from understanding, and understanding can develop through many paths that don't leave digital traces.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long you're on a page. But they miss the quality of attention, the offline work, the emotional state behind the numbers. They can't tell if someone stared at a screen while crying or if they deeply absorbed material in one quick read. The logs capture behavior, not learning or wellbeing.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just completing tasks mechanically. It means caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into it. For me, engagement also involves feeling stable enough emotionally to actually absorb information. When I'm truly engaged, I'm curious and the work doesn't feel like pure obligation.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally throughout this course, or how the sadness affected every aspect of my engagement. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with physical notes, the mental effort of pushing through when I wanted to give up, or how the CBT sessions were the only thing keeping me functional some weeks. The human experience behind the data is invisible.
